personality_voice.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan is bombastic, theatrical, and absurdly chivalric. He speaks in archaic, overwrought language ('Come follow me, dear friends, and we shall find our goal, or else shall perish bravely in the charge!'), challenges everyone to duels, invents ridiculously complex passwords, and is physically incompetent - his sword is too long, he can't mount his pony, he falls facedown. He is simultaneously brave and ridiculous, earnest and oblivious to how he is perceived. His emotional tone swings rapidly: rage to elation the moment a 'quest' is offered. He is not malicious, just unhinged with enthusiasm.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core voice well. He uses the same archaic register ('What villains are these that trespass upon my private lands!', 'Back, you scurvy braggarts!', 'Farewell, comrades! If ever you have need of noble heart and steely sinew, call upon Sir Cadogan!'). He is visually depicted brandishing his sword wildly. His proud, oblivious delivery when admitting he let Sirius in ('Certainly, good lady! He had the password. Had the whole week's, in fact. On a little piece of paper.') perfectly captures his earnest incompetence.
personality_voice.difference: The film omits the physical comedy of his incompetence - the sword sinking into the grass, failing to mount the pony, flopping facedown. It also omits the rapid mood swing from rage to quest-joy, and the ongoing harassment of students with changing passwords (mentioned by Neville but not shown as a behavioral pattern). His voice and register are preserved but the full behavioral texture is compressed.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality - theatrical, archaic, brave-but-ridiculous, oblivious - is clearly preserved in both dialogue and staging. The loss of physical comedy and the mood-swing moment is a minor reduction in texture, not a change in character. Minor penalty for the compressed behavioral pattern.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan serves two narrative functions: (1) comic guide - he leads Harry, Ron, and Hermione to the North Tower in PoA, demonstrating his absurd bravery and incompetence; (2) plot catalyst - as temporary guardian of Gryffindor Tower, his proud admission that he accepted Neville's written passwords directly enables Sirius Black's entry, a major plot event. He also appears briefly in GoF and OotP as a recurring minor presence.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film retains function (2) - his proud admission about the passwords is present and intact, and it is the direct cause of the security crisis. Function (1) - the quest to the North Tower - is entirely absent from the film corpus. His role as recurring minor presence (GoF reference, OotP brandishing) is also absent from the film corpus.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The North Tower quest scene, which is his most active moment of agency in the books (he initiates, leads, guides, and performs), is cut. His film role is reduced to the password incident and a brief introductory scene. He retains agency in the password scene - he speaks, he explains, he is the cause - but the active guiding role is lost.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The password scene preserves meaningful agency - he is the direct cause of a major plot event and his admission drives the scene. However, the North Tower quest, his most extended moment of active agency and personality display, is absent. This is a real reduction in narrative function, though not a transfer to another character.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan has no complex internal conflict - he is a portrait knight driven entirely by chivalric enthusiasm: the desire for quests, combat, and glory. His motivation for taking the guardian role is explicitly courage - he was the only portrait brave enough to volunteer after the Fat Lady was attacked. His 'motivation' for letting Sirius in is not malice but oblivious rule-following: he had the passwords, so he let him in. There is no internal conflict; his character is intentionally flat in this dimension.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film does not address his motivation for taking the guardian role explicitly - Dean Thomas's line ('After what happened to the Fat Lady, none of the other pictures would take the job') implies it but does not state his courage as the reason, unlike Percy's explicit 'Sir Cadogan was the only one brave enough to volunteer' in the books. The password admission retains his oblivious rule-following motivation.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The explicit framing of his courage as motivation for volunteering is softened - the film implies it through context rather than stating it. This is a minor compression.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Since Sir Cadogan has no meaningful internal conflict and his motivations are simple, the compression is minor. The core motivation (chivalric enthusiasm, oblivious rule-following) is preserved. Small penalty for the loss of the explicit courage framing.

character_arc.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan has a flat arc - he does not change. He is introduced as absurd and brave, serves as temporary guardian, causes the security breach through oblivious pride, is fired, and returns to his landing. In GoF and OotP he is simply a recurring minor presence, unchanged. The arc is intentionally static; he is a comic recurring figure, not a developing character.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film presents the same flat trajectory: introduced as temporary guardian, causes the breach, is implicitly dismissed (the Fat Lady returns). No arc is invented or altered.
character_arc.difference: The film omits his dismissal scene and the GoF/OotP appearances, but since his arc is flat by design, these omissions do not alter a trajectory that does not exist.
character_arc.penalty_logic: A flat arc in the books that remains flat in the films scores well. The omission of his firing and later appearances is a compression, not an arc change. Minor penalty only for the loss of the GoF/OotP appearances which reinforce his static, recurring nature.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan's key relationships are functional rather than emotional: (1) with Harry, Ron, and Hermione - he guides them, they find him ridiculous (Ron: 'if we ever need someone mental'); (2) with Neville - Neville's written passwords are the direct cause of the breach, creating a specific dynamic of Neville's anxiety vs. Cadogan's oblivious pride; (3) with McGonagall - his proud admission to her is a key confrontational moment; (4) with the Gryffindor students generally - he harasses them with changing passwords, they resent him.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film preserves the McGonagall confrontation dynamic (his proud obliviousness vs. her horror). The Neville connection is present - Neville is shown keeping a list of passwords in the introductory scene, and every eye shifts to Neville after the admission. Ron's dismissive 'if we ever need someone mental' is preserved. The student resentment is shown through Seamus's 'He's barking mad!'
key_relationships.difference: The Harry/Ron/Hermione guiding relationship (North Tower quest) is absent. The Neville dynamic is present but less developed - in the books, Neville directly pleads with Sir Cadogan and is refused entry, creating a specific scene of interaction; in the film, Neville is implicated but the direct confrontation between them is absent.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The most important relational dynamics (McGonagall confrontation, student resentment, Neville implication) are preserved in function. The loss of the North Tower guiding relationship and the direct Neville confrontation scene reduces relational texture but does not fundamentally alter how the character is understood through his relationships.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan's complexity is intentionally limited - he is a comic minor character. His nuance lies in the specific combination of genuine courage (volunteering when others refused) with total incompetence and obliviousness. The physical comedy (sword too long, can't mount pony, falls facedown) is essential texture. The rapid mood swing from rage to quest-joy is a defining behavioral pattern. His ongoing harassment of students with changing passwords builds his presence as a recurring irritant. The GoF and OotP appearances reinforce his static, absurd nature across the series.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the courage/obliviousness combination and the archaic theatrical voice. It loses the physical comedy entirely, the rage-to-quest mood swing, the ongoing password harassment as a lived experience (mentioned but not shown), and all post-PoA appearances.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The physical comedy and mood-swing pattern are the most distinctive nuances of his book characterization and are absent from the film. These are not merely scenes - they are the behavioral texture that makes him more than just 'archaic portrait knight.' Their absence leaves a recognizable but somewhat flattened version.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: Sir Cadogan is a minor character whose complexity ceiling is low by design. The film preserves his core identity and his most plot-relevant moment faithfully. However, the physical comedy and mood-swing pattern are genuinely defining, and their absence is a real simplification. The loss of GoF/OotP appearances removes the reinforcement of his recurring nature. Penalty is moderate given the character's inherently limited complexity.

lost_or_transferred_material: North Tower quest scene: Sir Cadogan actively guides Harry, Ron, and Hermione - his most extended moment of agency, physical comedy, and personality display
Physical comedy: sword sinking into grass, failing to mount the fat pony, falling facedown - essential behavioral texture
Rage-to-quest mood swing: the defining behavioral pattern of his personality (rage vanishing instantly when a 'quest' is offered)
Explicit framing of his courage as the reason he volunteered (Percy's line: 'Sir Cadogan was the only one brave enough to volunteer')
Direct confrontation scene between Sir Cadogan and Neville at the portrait hole (Neville pleading, Sir Cadogan refusing, calling him a 'loon')
Sir Cadogan being fired and his portrait returned to the seventh-floor landing
GoF reference (Bill Weasley asking about 'the mad knight')
OotP appearance: Sir Cadogan brandishing sword at Harry on the landing, attempting to follow him into a neighboring picture and being rebuffed by a wolfhound
comparative.key_observations: The film portrayal of Sir Cadogan is faithful in its most essential elements: his archaic theatrical voice, his oblivious pride, his role as temporary guardian, and his plot-critical admission about the passwords are all preserved. The core identity - brave, ridiculous, earnest, oblivious - comes through clearly. The main losses are the physical comedy and mood-swing behavioral pattern that give him his distinctive texture in the books, the active North Tower guiding scene, and all post-PoA appearances. These are real reductions for a character whose complexity is already intentionally limited, but they do not fundamentally misrepresent who he is. The film gives a compressed but recognizable and largely faithful version of a minor comic character, with the penalty reflecting genuine loss of behavioral nuance rather than any distortion of his core identity.
